plan.contextChunk:
y &= f \frac{Y}{Z} 
\end{align}$${#eq-perspctiveProj}

:::{.column-margin}
Thales of Miletus, 624 B.C., introduced the notion of **similar triangles**.  It seems he used this to measure the height of Egypt pyramids, and the distance to boats in the sea.
:::

Equations @eq-perspctiveProj1 and @eq-perspctiveProj are called the **perspective projection
equations**. Under perspective projection, distant objects become
smaller, through the inverse scaling by $Z$. As we will see, the
perspective projection equations apply not just to pinhole cameras but
to most lens-based cameras, and human vision as well.

![Perspective projection equations derived geometrically. From similar
triangles, we have $x/f = X/Z$ and $y/f = Y/Z$. Similar triangles are
indicated by the same color.](figures/imaging/similar_triangles2.png){#fig-pinholeGeometry2
width="80%"}

Due to the choice of coordinate systems, the coordinates in the virtual
camera plane have the $x$ coordinate in the opposite direction than the
way we usually do for image coordinates $(m,n)$, where $m$ indexes the
pixel column and $n$ the pixel row in an image. This is shown in @fig-pinholeGeometry (b). The
relationship between camera coordinates and image coordinates is
$$\begin{aligned}
n &= - a x + n_0\\
m &= a y + m_0
\label{eq-cameratoimagecoordinates}
\end{aligned}$$ where $a$ is a constant, and $(n_0, m_0)$ is the image
coordinates of the camera optical axis. Note that this is different than
what we introduced a simple projection model in in the framework of the
simple vision system in @sec-simplesystem. In that example, we placed the world

elements: pinhole (aperture) at the origin
3D point P at (X, Y, Z) shown as a red sphere
projection (image) plane perpendicular to the Z-axis at distance f
projected point p = (x, y) marked on the image plane as a brown dot
dashed line from 3D point P through the pinhole to projected point p
X, Y, Z coordinate axes (with arrowheads and labels)
red-highlighted similar triangle in the XZ plane
cyan-highlighted similar triangle in the YZ plane
green-highlighted triangle connecting P, pinhole, and projected point
dimension labels: x, y, X, Y, Z, f
vertical and horizontal green construction lines from p to axes on image plane
image plane semi-transparent shading
colored sector fills for similar-triangle regions

interactions.label: P: Depth Z
interactions.teaches: Shows how moving P in depth affects the projected (x, y) location and triangle proportions, demonstrating inverse scaling by Z.

interactions.label: P: X
interactions.teaches: Shows how moving P laterally (X) moves the projection p horizontally, visualizing x = fX/Z.

interactions.label: P: Y
interactions.teaches: Shows how moving P vertically (Y) moves the projection p vertically, visualizing y = fY/Z.

interactions.label: Focal length f
interactions.teaches: Adjusts the projection plane distance, demonstrating how f acts as a zoom, controlling magnification on the image plane.

interactions.label: Show similar triangles
interactions.teaches: Toggles the highlight of the colored similar triangles to reinforce how the projection equations arise from triangle proportions.

demo_steps.title: Basic setup: P in front of pinhole
demo_steps.narration: Here's a 3D point P at (X=4, Y=3, Z=8) and a focal length f=2. The dashed line connects P through the pinhole to its image p on the projection plane. Colored shapes show how similar triangles underlie the projection equations.

demo_steps.title: Moving deeper: Objects shrink
demo_steps.narration: Slide P farther away to Z=15. Notice that the projection p moves closer to the optical axis and the similar triangles flatten, shrinking x and y. This is the hallmark of perspective: twice as far means half as large.

demo_steps.title: Offset in X: Linear in X
demo_steps.narration: Change P's X position to -6 while holding Z and Y fixed. The projected x coordinate now shifts to the other side of the optical axis, showing directly how x responds linearly to X while Z and f remain constant.

demo_steps.title: Changing vertical Y
demo_steps.narration: Set Y to -5, putting P below the optical axis. The projected p now appears lower on the image plane, perfectly in proportion to the negative Y value due to y = fY/Z.

demo_steps.title: Magnification: Varying f
demo_steps.narration: Increase f to 5. The projection plane moves farther from the pinhole, and p shifts further out. The similar triangles grow taller, showing how a longer focal length magnifies the image—just like zooming in with a lens.

demo_steps.title: Focus on triangles
demo_steps.narration: Toggle off the triangle highlights. With only the rays and geometry left, picture for yourself how the triangles nest between P, the pinhole, and p—these are what Thales used to relate distant heights and positions by sight.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Oblique 3D view along the positive diagonal (from x=−y, z up) with slight elevation, so both the projection plane and the 3D point’s movement are visible. Center the pinhole at the origin and keep projection plane fully in view as f varies.
interactionPlan.notes: Update lines, projected points, and triangle overlays reactively as sliders move. Use semi-transparent colored Mesh objects for the triangle fills; highlight axis arrows and dimension lines for clarity. All labels should be readable from the oblique view. When showTriangles is off, hide only the colored regions; keep construction lines and main geometry visible.

attempts.html:
Basic setup
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: In the rendered Three.js view, the overall size of the 3D diagram is much smaller than optimal, making geometry and labels hard to read.
The red and cyan triangle fills look somewhat less vivid and less distinct compared to the 2D source, with triangle alignment slightly off in perspective.
The interactive version's construction lines and axis extensions are less visually prominent and not as smoothly integrated as the hand-drawn source image.
The label placement is more cramped and sometimes overlaps or is difficult to read given the tiny 3D canvas.
The image plane and its border are represented but lack the heavier dark emphasis of the source figure.
evaluation.notes: Highly interactive and accurate in concept, but small scale, crowded labels, and less clear triangle overlays reduce fidelity and clarity.
action_items: Increase the default size/scaling of the 3D scene so that all geometry and labels are clearly visible and not cramped.
Tweak the label positions, size, and background for better readability and separation—especially x, y, X, Y, and f.
Enhance triangle overlay opacities and colors to more closely match the source and improve visual distinction.
Make projection plane edges bolder/darker to improve correspondence with the source figure.

feedback.rationale: All issues concern rendering quality, visibility, color treatment, and label placement, while the conceptual decomposition and interactivity are correct.
action_items: Increase the default size/scaling of the 3D scene so that all geometry and labels are clearly visible and not cramped.
Tweak the label positions, size, and background for better readability and separation—especially x, y, X, Y, and f.
Enhance triangle overlay opacities and colors to more closely match the source and improve visual distinction.
Make projection plane edges bolder/darker to improve correspondence with the source figure.
actionItems: Increase the default size/scaling of the 3D scene so that all geometry and labels are clearly visible and not cramped.
Tweak the label positions, size, and background for better readability and separation—especially x, y, X, Y, and f.
Enhance triangle overlay opacities and colors to more closely match the source and improve visual distinction.
Make projection plane edges bolder/darker to improve correspondence with the source figure.

attempts.html:
Basic setup
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The label for the image plane is 'image plane z=f' with blue font, which differs from the plain italic 'f' (with no z=f label) in the source figure.
The interactive figure includes additional guide lines and edge tubes not visible in the source figure.
The reference uses a large brown dot for the projected point on the image plane, while the generated figure uses a smaller brown sphere.
Label positions and fonts differ, with the interactive version often using boxed, multi-line, or differently colored text boxes versus the more sparse, typographically elegant source.
The interactive UI contains projection equations (x/f = X/Z, y/f = Y/Z) and narration not present in the static source.
evaluation.notes: Interactive figure closely matches the original geometry and concept and adds highly useful pedagogical interactivity, but labels and some UI elements diverge visually from the static figure.
action_items: Refine label formats, font sizes, and placements to more closely resemble the typographic style and minimalism of the original figure.
Adjust the color and style of 3D primitives (dots, arrows, filled triangles) to more exactly mirror the original—consider a larger, opaque brown dot for the projected point.
Simplify or make optional some of the figure's helper lines and equation callouts to declutter and improve visual faithfulness.
Allow toggling or customizing of extraneous annotations and UI so the bare 3D construction view can precisely match the original textbook figure for use in print/export.

feedback.rationale: The plan is conceptually correct and covers all necessary elements, but the execution needs refinement in labels, visual styles, and optional UI elements to improve faithfulness to the source.
action_items: Refine label formats, font sizes, and placements to more closely resemble the typographic style and minimalism of the original figure.
Adjust the color and style of 3D primitives (dots, arrows, filled triangles) to more exactly mirror the original—consider a larger, opaque brown dot for the projected point.
Simplify or make optional some of the figure's helper lines and equation callouts to declutter and improve visual faithfulness.
Allow toggling or customizing of extraneous annotations and UI so the bare 3D construction view can precisely match the original textbook figure for use in print/export.

Allow toggling or customizing of extraneous annotations and UI so the bare 3D construction view can precisely match the original textbook figure for use in print/export.

attempts.html:
Demo
Basic setup
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The X, Y, Z axis arrows in the generated figure are bolder and in different orientation/length than the original.
The source figure has the image plane slightly slanted and with a semi-transparent gray shade; the generated figure shows it upright with a blue tint and label.
Green and cyan triangle fills are present in the generated figure, but the arrangement and proportions do not precisely match the 2D source triangles (especially edge composition and overlays).
Vector lines, especially the green and red outlines, are thicker and more visually dominant than in the source.
Some label sizes, positions, and detail (e.g., 'p = (x, y)' vs just 'x, y') are not identical to the original.
evaluation.notes: The interactive figure is highly functional and conceptually accurate, but diverges in geometric composition, triangle placements, and some label/layout visual fidelity.
action_items: Tweak the orientation and color/transparency of the image plane to better match the original (slanted, grayish).
Refine the triangle fills and outlines so their proportions and overlays mimic the source's 2D relationships more closely.
Adjust axis arrows for thickness, length, and slight perspective consistency.
Reposition and standardize label fonts/sizes for closer match to the source, and clarify whether labels like 'p = (x, y)' are needed.
Fine-tune the border thickness and coloring of all construction lines (especially green/red/cyan) to more subtly echo the original.

feedback.rationale: All action items and discrepancies concern geometric accuracy, visual fidelity, labeling, and styling adjustments rather than conceptual or structural errors in the plan.
action_items: Tweak the orientation and color/transparency of the image plane to better match the original (slanted, grayish).
Refine the triangle fills and outlines so their proportions and overlays mimic the source's 2D relationships more closely.
Adjust axis arrows for thickness, length, and slight perspective consistency.
Reposition and standardize label fonts/sizes for closer match to the source, and clarify whether labels like 'p = (x, y)' are needed.
Fine-tune the border thickness and coloring of all construction lines (especially green/red/cyan) to more subtly echo the original.
